behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: The real failure boundary is the backoff calculation itself (pure deterministic math/state progression). No I/O or cross-module wiring is required to expose incorrect doubling or cap logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test a pure backoff calculator (or retry-state object) with a configured base delay and max delay; simulate consecutive failures and assert the produced delay sequence doubles each time and clamps at the max.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: A pure mock of decision logic can miss failures in wiring between HTTP response parsing and retry policy (e.g., status/header extraction mismatches). Minimum confidence requires multiple modules wired together.
behaviors.test_description: Run the client with real retry middleware + HTTP adapter layer against controlled responses (e.g., 429 with retry metadata). Assert the library classifies it as rate-limit and computes/schedules the expected wait.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This depends on orchestration across timer/backoff, request replay, and response propagation. Unit tests of isolated functions can miss replay/wiring failures.
behaviors.test_description: Use fake timers plus a controlled transport stub: first response is rate-limited, second is success. Assert `call()` waits, reissues the same request payload, and resolves to the success result without exposing retry internals to the caller.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Typed error guarantees can fail at module boundaries (retry engine -> public API surface). A pure unit test may miss rewrapping/serialization mistakes.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries, force repeated rate-limit responses, and assert `call()` rejects with `QuotaExhaustedError` (instance/type guard) and not a generic `Error`.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Termination bugs typically emerge from interaction between retry counters, quota state, and scheduler/timer behavior. Cross-module execution is needed to catch real infinite-loop risks.
behaviors.test_description: Drive the real retry pipeline with persistent rate-limit responses under fake time; assert bounded attempt count, bounded virtual elapsed time, and eventual rejection with the terminal quota error.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: This is a shared-state concurrency contract across callers/modules. In-process mocks can hide race and isolation bugs unless the real quota manager wiring is exercised.
behaviors.test_description: Launch concurrent `call()` invocations against one process/account context with a deterministic quota limit. Assert aggregate consumption reflects one shared bucket (not per-caller buckets) and excess calls are retried/blocked consistently.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: FIFO dispatch is a queueing/scheduler behavior that depends on async coordination among modules; isolated unit mocks can miss ordering regressions under contention.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple calls quickly while dispatch is rate-limited; capture send order at the transport boundary and assert it matches submission order exactly.
